title: Anger and Short‑Fuse Days
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Anger is often a stress signal. Catch it at yellow, not red.

content.text: Anger is usually the visible layer of a deeper load stack: fatigue, pain, threat activation, and unresolved stress.
When you are overloaded, the gap between trigger and reaction shrinks. Small events get treated like major threats.

items: Jaw clench, shoulder tension, or heat rise
Urge to dominate, shut down, or leave hard

sections.heading: Why Anger Spikes on Certain Days

items: Unrepaired conflict from earlier in week

lines: Why this works: early interoceptive cue awareness increases inhibitory control and reduces aggressive outbursts.

items.text: walk, wall push, or cold-water reset to burn activation.
buy 90 seconds before speaking on charged topics.

items: Name the impact on the other person.
State one specific behavior you will change.

lines: Repair sentence template: 'I came in too hot. That was not okay. Next time I will call yellow and reset before speaking.'

items: Escalating alcohol or drug use tied to anger
Frequent remorse with no sustained behavior change

content.text: Each day log one trigger, one early cue, and whether you used the 90-second interrupt before saying something you regret.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Anger and Short-Fuse Days - Fuse Tracker
worksheets.intro: Track anger patterns and practice interrupting escalation before red-zone behavior.

question_groups.label: What triggered your anger spikes this week?
question_groups.hint: List event, context, and load factors (sleep, hunger, pain, stress).

question_groups.label: What did anger cost you?
question_groups.hint: Include impact on trust, connection, decisions, and self-respect.

question_groups.label: What interrupt will you use this week?
question_groups.hint: Choose one script + one body reset to run consistently.

numbered_prompts.hint: What signal told you your fuse was shortening?
What did you do in the first 90 seconds?
Did intensity drop or continue rising?
If anger leaked, what specific repair did you complete?

worksheets.closing_note: Progress is measured by fewer red events and faster repairs.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Anger and Short-Fuse Days - Repair Plan
worksheets.intro: Use this with partner/family to make post-anger repair specific and consistent.

question_groups.label: What anger behaviors cause most damage?
question_groups.hint: List the top two behaviors to eliminate first.

question_groups.label: What repair does each person need?
question_groups.hint: Ask directly and write it plainly.

question_groups.label: What is your no-excuses repair protocol?
question_groups.hint: Define timing, wording, and behavior follow-through.

numbered_prompts.hint: Write the exact words you will use.
What concrete behavior changes this week?
When will you verify that repair landed?

worksheets.closing_note: Trust returns faster when repair is specific and repeated.

paragraphs: Anger is information. It tells you your system is loaded and your safety margin is thin.
The goal is not to never feel anger. The goal is to act skillfully before anger causes damage.
closing.highlight: Catch yellow early and anger becomes manageable instead of destructive.
